sections.title: Language Study
sections.subtitle:
Grammar
Dictation
Penmanship & Copywork
Written Narration
sections.subject: English

entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Grammar: Grade 4, Dictation: Grade 4, Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 4, Written Narration: Grade 4

About Grammar: Grade 4
Students learn the parts of speech through a four-part analysis.

About Dictation: Grade 4
Students use selected passages to practice dictation skills. (Alveary Copywork & Dictation Books provide selected passages).

About Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 4
Students copy short passages from their books. (Alveary Copywork & Dictation Books provide selected passages).

About Written Narration: Grade 4
Students begin to make written narrations a weekly habit.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
Language Study: Grade 4
Grade 4 students who did not complete all three levels of the Alveary Reading Lessons, who are having difficulty with spelling, who cannot read well enough to narrate, or who make more than 10 errors in a 100-word passage should continue the reading lessons while the teacher dials back on the amount of reading required in other subjects.

Written Narration: Grade 4
Students who are not yet secure with their penmanship are not yet ready for written narration. Instead of written narrations, use the time to have students work on skills that will better support their development. Also, be sure to include typing in life skills!

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 4
If you are using the Getty-Dubay Italic Handwriting Series, complete half a page per day or go at student's pace.

Written Narration: Grade 4
☐ Plan to complete one written narration every week and consider increasing to two by the end of the year. 

☐ Written narrations can happen after reading at the end of any lesson if time allows and do not need to take up an extra 10 minutes in your schedule. This tracker serves to help you keep records of what was done throughout the year.

☐ To keep written narrations fresh and interesting, you can try different ways to have your students narrate what they read or observed outside. In addition to just “what did we read about?”, you can ask students to “write the story of ___.” (Example: “Write the story of Loki.”) Some students may also like to imagine they are a character in the story and write what happened as a diary entry (Example: "Pretend you are Christian in Pilgrim’s Progress and write a letter or diary entry telling what happened to him in today’s chapter.”)

entries.title: Term Prep & Teacher Tips
entries.content:
Grammar: Grade 4
☐ Bookmark or Print Grammar Island Additional Practice PDF (Find in Quick Links)

☐ Bookmark Grammar Island Teacher Manual select pages for lesson answer key (Find in Quick Links)

☐ Bookmark Practice Island Teacher Manual for answer key (Find in Quick Links)

Written Narration: Grade 4
Some ideas on helping students transition to written narration: 
☐ Write down words on a whiteboard if a student wants to use a word they don’t know how to spell.
☐ Have students write down brief sentences (even numbered statements) telling the sequence of events, and then have them orally give more details about each of them.
☐ Write the beginning and then orally tell the rest, or vice versa.

links.label: Grammar Island Additional Practice PDF Answer Key
Grammar Island Teacher Manual select pages for lesson answer key
Practice Island Teacher Manual for answer key
Grammar Island Pg. Numbers/Beginning Ending Quotes Ebook

blocks.text: Select a passage that has already been read and narrated. (We provide passages in the Dictation packets.) Have students read over it and select 4-5 words that they don't know. They can also mark punctuation that may be difficult to remember. They only need to select a section that contains 4-5 new words. If this is only a couple of sentences, that is fine.

Ask students which words they think will be most difficult to spell and why. If there is a common spelling pattern, you can point that out. You can also point out other words that have a similar pattern.
Have students visualize the words by "seeing" them with their eyes closed. They should also practice on a whiteboard.
As part of the same lesson, when students feel ready, slowly call out a sentence from what they studied. You will want to read it phrase by phrase and not repeat it.
When finished, have students look over their own work to check and see that everything looks right. If you see any misspelled words, erase those and have the students practice those again.
Link back to the previous lesson.

Read the assigned pages and discuss new ideas. It is important for students to be able to talk about the concepts in their own words. The pictures and diagrams in the book can serve as a springboard for discussions about the grammar concepts.
Complete practice examples in the text and/or the practice book that accompany the lesson.
Observe examples of concepts in books being read and in daily life.

lessons.title: 10m Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 4 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Tracker
⬚ ⬚ ⬚ ⬚ ⬚
⍞ Materials: Grade 4 Student packet of copywork and dictation passages

➜ Spend ten minutes daily working on copywork this week. You can use this week's suggested passage or choose your own of a similar length. Spend the first 3-5 minutes of your copywork lesson focusing on penmanship by practicing a few letters in print or cursive from the Penny Gardner book or another style of your choice.

lessons.title: 15m Dictation: Grade 4 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Tracker
⬚ ⬚ ⬚
⍞ Materials: Grade 4 Student packet of copywork and dictation passages 

➜ Three Dictation lessons are scheduled each week. In each lesson, there should be time for studying (10 min) and time for the sentences to be dictated (5 min). You can use this week's suggested passage for the lessons and/or choose your own generally from the history, literature, or geography books the student has already read during the week. The suggested passage might take one lesson to learn, or it might take all three lessons to learn. It will be different for each student. Be sure to pay attention to any notes regarding grammar or punctuation added to the student dictation packet for the week.

Enough text to include 4-5 unknown words should be studied by the student. This might mean that the student is only studying a couple of sentences. Ask for the words they do not know how to spell. Write them down on a chalkboard, whiteboard, or piece of paper. The student should work on visualizing each word until they can see it with their eyes shut, and then practice writing the word, drawing the letters in the air, or saying the letters aloud until it is memorized.

Discuss any interesting spelling patterns or punctuation from the text being studied. The process should take around 10 minutes to this point.

Then, choose a sentence or two to slowly dictate a few words at a time to the student from the text studied. Read it once. Correct any errors and revisit any words missed.  

Be sure to adapt the amount studied and dictated to suit the student. Add to or subtract from the amount or difficulty of the text studied to ensure the student's success and growth.

lessons.title: 10m Written Narration: Grade 4 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
➜ Complete one written narration this week. Make sure to write on a variety of different subjects, either over the term or at least over the year.

➜ NOTE 
Written narrations can happen at the end of any lesson if time allows and do not need to take up an extra 10 minutes in your schedule. This tracker serves to help you keep records of what was done throughout the year.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Note that students who are not yet secure with their penmanship are not yet ready for written narration. Instead of written narrations, use the time to have students work on skills that will better support their development, such as climbing on trees or playground equipment, doing household chores, working with clay or dough, and doing mazes and dot-to-dot activities. Also, be sure to include typing in life skills!

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Introduction  
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
Did you know that sentences do not occur in nature? Language comes from us; it is ourselves, in words!

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.5-8 

➜ DISCUSS
How is a word different from a random group of letters, like "dixerp?"
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
 The key to explaining the four levels of grammar revolves around the noun and the verb. 

Parts of Speech: Noun and Verb
Parts of Sentence: Subject and Predicate
Phrases: No Subject Predicate allowed
Clauses: Subject Predicate required

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
What Is Grammar? 
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.9-11

➜ DISCUSS
Think about a team sport. How are the players different from the game? How could this idea be applied to language?

lessons.title: 10m Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 4 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Tracker
⬚ ⬚ ⬚ ⬚ ⬚
⍞ Materials: Grade 4 Student packet of copywork and dictation passages

➜ Spend 3-5 minutes on penmanship and then work on completing passages from your copywork/dictation book for the rest of your ten minutes.

lessons.title: 15m Dictation: Grade 4 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Tracker
⬚ ⬚ ⬚
⍞ Materials: Grade 4 Student packet of copywork and dictation passages 

➜ Spend three days this week working on a complete dictation lesson (10 min. of study and 5 min. for passage to be dictated). You can use this week's suggested passage or choose your own of a similar length. Be sure to pay attention to any added notes in the student dictation packet for the week. Only choose enough text so the student learns 4-5 new words at a time.

lessons.title: 10m Written Narration: Grade 4 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
➜ Complete one written narration this week.

➜ NOTE
If the student is new to written narration, they may fatigue quickly. Allow them to initially write a bit, and tell the rest. Some may do better by first orally narrating and discussing as usual, then writing a sentence or so of that narration. Others may do better by beginning their narration in writing, then completing it orally.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
One way to help students transition to written narrations is to provide external supports before writing, such as index cards that can be arranged and re-ordered. You can also encourage students to talk aloud as they write or use speech-to-text and then edit.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
The Parts of Speech  
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
What do we mean when we say the "parts of a bird"? [the small bits that make up the whole bird] Our language has parts that make up the whole, too.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.13-19 

➜ DIAGRAM
Copy the diagram on p.17. Color the parts with crayons or colored pencils to match the drawing. Which parts seem most important?

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Nouns 
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ RECAP
What are the two main kinds of words? [nouns and verbs]

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.20-21 

➜ PLAY
What would it be like if you could only talk in nouns? Try it! Can it work? [Remember, you can only use words that NAME things!]

lessons.teacherNotes:

Make sure students are practicing typing skills regularly, and have them try typing a narration occasionally.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
How do we know when to use the word "I" and when to use "me"? Think of a sentence with each.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.22-24 

➜ DISCUSS
The prefix "pro" means "for." A "Pro-noun" is a word you use "for a noun." "Ante" means "before," and "cede" means "to go." So, the noun that "went before" the pronoun is called its "antecedent."
Read the left column on p.24 aloud and time it; read the right column aloud and time it. Pronouns are quick!

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Subject Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.25-27 

➜ WRITE
Copy the lists of subject pronouns and object pronouns. Look at the last narration you wrote. Did you use any pronouns? Circle them with an aqua pencil.

★ TEACHER TIP
If students have a hard time writing narrations using their books, try asking them to write about their morning or what they saw on a nature walk.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
Nouns & Pronouns
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
Two of the subject pronouns are "he" and "she." How do we know when to use which?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.28-29 

➜ DISCUSS
"She" is a feminine pronoun, so it needs to replace a feminine noun.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Adjectives
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ INTRO
Quick! List as many kinds of tables as you can think of in one minute! Can you imagine if there was a whole separate noun for each one of these?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.30-33

➜ DISCUSS
Good writers use adjectives when they are needed. Too many adjectives make sentences too wordy and spoil the story.
Adjectives do not "describe" nouns from a distance but actually change them. The old car is a different car from the sports car. Adjectives modify.

⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ PRACTICE
Complete the activity on p.34 of Grammar Island. Why are some adjectives funny and others not funny?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.35-36 

➜ PRACTICE
Draw a purple box around each of the seven adjectives that modify the word "duck."

lessons.body:
Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.50-52 

➜ DISCUSS
On p.52, there are two sentences about ducks. One shows what the duck DOES. The other shows what the duck EQUALS. Can you figure out which is which?

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
Action Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.53-55 

➜ PRACTICE
For each subject, provide an action verb that makes sense:
1. The kangaroo
2. Mom
3. A lion
4. Our car

Linking Verbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.56-60 

➜ DISCUSS
In the sentence, "Sam has a hamster," why is "has" an action verb and not a linking verb?

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
Linking Verbs (cont.)
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.61-62 

➜ PRACTICE
Follow the instructions to identify the action and linking verbs. Try replacing the verb with "=" to test.
Grammar Island p.63. Check your answers on p.64.

Adverbs
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ RECAP
Some words, like "smells," can be either an action verb or a linking verb. How can that be? [ex. He smells a steak. / He smells good.] 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.65-69  

➜ PRACTICE
Label the noun, verb, adjective, and adverb in the sentence on p.69.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 21
lessons.body:
Conjunctions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ INTRO
What is a "junction"? [a place where things come together, e.g., roads, railroads, etc.] Today's lesson is about a type of word that brings other words together.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.75-80 

➜ DISCUSS
What would our language sound like if we did not have conjunctions? Try it with the sentences on p.79.
On p.80, why does the third pair make a good conjunction when the first two do not?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
For more practice with Conjunctions, see the PDF.
∞ PDF Link: Grammar Island Additional Practice

➜ Complete one written narration this week. Make sure to write on a variety of different subjects, either over the term or at least over the year.

➜ NOTE 
To keep written narrations fresh and interesting, you can try different ways to have your students narrate what they read or observed outside. In addition to just “what did we read about?”, you can ask students to “write the story of ___.” (Example: “Write the story of Loki.”) 

Some students may also like to imagine they are a character in the story and write what happened as a diary entry. (Example: "Pretend you are Christian in Pilgrim’s Progress and write a letter or diary entry telling what happened to him in today’s chapter.”)
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Written narrations can happen at the end of any lesson if time allows, and do not need to take up an extra 10 minutes in your schedule. This tracker serves to help you keep records of what was done throughout the year.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
Prepositions
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.81-84  

➜ PRACTICE
Place a pencil somewhere in the room. Say three sentences to describe where the pencil is in space. What are the prepositions you used? Could you do it without using prepositions?

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
Prepositional Phrases
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ INTRO
Read the sentences on p.82 again. Notice the rhythm of the words in each prepositional phrase. Try clapping the rhythm.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.85-87 

➜ RECITE
Do you remember the object pronouns? Recite as many as you can. If you need help, look back on p.26.

lessons.body:

➜ Spend 3-5 minutes on penmanship and then work on completing passages from your copywork/dictation book for the rest of your ten minutes.

lessons.title: 10m Written Narration: Grade 4 - Lesson 14
lessons.body: ➜ Complete one written narration this week.
lessons.teacherNotes:

Make sure students are practicing typing skills regularly, and have them try typing a narration occasionally.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
Interjections
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.88-89

➜ DISCUSS
Interjections are unique; they do not interact with other words. They do not modify, replace, or join. Why would "yes" and "no" be interjections?
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
If interested, let students write their own story or do it together. Pick a part of speech, and then write a story that illustrates the nature of that part.

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
Putting the Parts Together
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
•Go over each sentence on p.90-91 slowly and carefully, noticing how each word is labeled.
•Read p.92-93 

➜ PRACTICE
Since Bertie could only hear modifiers, she only heard parts of what was said to her. Go back and figure out what she heard.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
For more practice with the eight parts of speech, see the PDF.
∞ PDF Link: Grammar Island Additional Practice

For groups or classrooms,
∞ PDF Link: 2 group games for Grammar Island

• Remember to use the "=" test to identify linking verbs and subject complements.
• Why do we understand the subject more completely when there is a subject complement?

lessons.body:
Clauses
⍞ Materials: Grammar Island
 
➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Grammar Island p.137-141

➜ PRACTICE
Write a sentence that has two clauses.

➜ NOTE
A key concept is that the phrases are INSIDE the clauses. The clause includes the phrase. The phrase is a part of its clause.

lessons.body:
Tracker
⬚ ⬚ ⬚
⍞ Materials: Grade 4 Student packet of copywork and dictation passages 

➜ Spend three days this week working on dictation.

lessons.title: 10m Written Narration: Grade 4 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
➜ Complete one written narration this week. Make sure to write on a variety of different subjects, either over the term or at least over the year.

➜ NOTE 
To keep written narrations fresh and interesting, you can try different ways to have your students narrate what they read or observed outside. In addition to just “what did we read about?”, you can ask students to “write the story of ___.” (Example: “Write the story of Loki.”) 

Some students may also like to imagine they are a character in the story and write what happened as a diary entry (Example: "Pretend you are Christian in Pilgrim’s Progress and write a letter or diary entry telling what happened to him in today’s chapter.”)
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER TIP
Note that students who are not yet secure with their penmanship are not yet ready for written narration. Instead of written narrations, use the time to have students work on skills that will better support their development, such as climbing on trees or playground equipment, doing household chores, working with clay or dough, and doing mazes and dot-to-dot activities. Also, be sure to include typing in life skills!

lessons.title: 15m Grammar: Grade 4 - Lesson 49

⍞ Materials: Practice Island
 
➜ PRACTICE
Label parts of speech, parts of the sentence, phrases, and clauses. Write a comment. Check your work. Practice Island Sentences 1-2  
∞ PDF Link: Practice Island Answer Key
lessons.teacherNotes: Go at your student's pace as you work together through these sentences. It is better to take the time to talk through each sentence and ensure the student understands each part than to get through lots of sentences with a lower level of understanding.

terms.content:
Grammar: Grade 4
· Grammar Island: Five Kinds of Words: Can you tell the part of speech of every word in these sentences? Put n. below each noun, pron. below each pronoun, adj. below each adjective, v. below each verb, and adv. below each adverb.

She is nice.


Polly wants a cracker badly.


The eagle chick was fuzzy.


A high cloud suddenly dropped blue rain.

Dictation: Grade 4
· Dictate an unstudied passage with words similar in difficulty to what was dictated during the term.

Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 4
· Write two lines of poetry from memory.

· Share your Copywork Journal with an adult.

Grammar: Grade 4
· Practice Island: Is the word in brackets a noun, pronoun, adjective, verb, adverb, preposition, conjunction, or interjection? 
_______ 1. Many fish circled in the blue water [under] the dock. 
_______ 2. A [flock]of birds passed high over the island, flying south. 
_______ 3. [The] sunset spread red and purple light over the sky. 
_______ 4. [Yes], the heavy seas pounded the rocks on the north shore. 
_______ 5. A frog sang [happily] in the grass at the edge of the sand. 
Is the word in brackets a subject, predicate, direct object, or subject complement? 
_______ 6. Grammar Island is a small [place], rocky and covered with pines. 
_______ 7. A [school] of shiny silver fish jumped from the aqua waves. 
_______ 8. The squirrel [chattered] noisily from the high branch. 
_______ 9. An owl hooted in the moonlight, and we saw his [shadow]. 
_______ 10. It was a peaceful [night], and the water lapped gently at the shore. 
Is the part in brackets a phrase or a clause? 
_______ 11. The big waves rolled [over the rocks], exploding into foam. 
_______ 12. [Grammar Island is a good home for many small animals]. 
_______ 13. [The ducks flew south this morning], and we watched them take off. 
_______ 14. [Birds chirped]. 
_______ 15. The sandy beach was a good place to run [in the morning sun].

Dictation: Grade 4
· Dictate an unstudied passage with words similar in difficulty to what was dictated during the term.

Penmanship & Copywork: Grade 4
· Write two lines of poetry from memory.

· Share your Copywork Journal with an adult.
